data.title: Yau Tong project sells out, raising hopes of market recovery amid rate cuts, rising demand
data.description:
A residential project in Yau Tong triggered buying euphoria on Saturday, with all 150 units snapped up by midafternoon amid heightened expectations for a recovery in Hong Kong’s home market next year.
The flats at One Park Place, priced between HK$4.23 million (US$543,684) and HK$9.51 million, were in high demand after developers Sino Land, CSI Properties and MTR Corporation offered discounts of up to 15 per cent to lure buyers.
The strong homebuying interest came a day after New World Development (NWD) sold out all 63 units at its Austin Bohemian project in Yau Ma Tei on Friday.
“The units appeared to be attractive not only to residents who wanted to own them for self-use, but buyers looking to own them for investment purposes,” said Sammy Po Siu-ming, senior director at Midland Realty. “Since the rent is estimated at HK$50 per sq foot per month, the annualised investment return could reach 4 per cent.”
Po said one client of Midland shelled out HK$25 million to purchase five units.
The One Park Place flats were originally scheduled to be offered on November 28, two days after a deadly fire broke out in the Wang Fuk Court residential complex in Tai Po, but developers postponed the sale as the city focused on rescue and relief operations.
About 60 per cent of the flats, measuring from 293 sq ft to 578 sq ft, would be for self-use, according to Po.
Hong Kong home prices are likely to see an uptick of 5 per cent in 2026, buoyed by demand from mainland Chinese buyers, interest rate cuts and fewer unsold new flats, according to analysts.
The stamp duty adjustment and Hong Kong’s resurgent stock market were also expected to help sustain stability in the city’s residential property market, executives with JLL and Cushman & Wakefield said recently.
The city’s residential market has experienced seven years of volatility, as prices hit a peak in July 2018, declined and then climbed back to another peak in September 2021, after which they fell nearly 30 per cent, according to official data.
Lived-in home prices were down as much as 28.4 per cent in March this year from their peak in September 2021. That slump came amid elevated inventory levels, which required 101.6 months to clear in 2023 and 67.4 months in 2024.
With home sales up by more than a fifth on an annual basis as of November, unsold inventory had declined, according to JLL.
It said the unsold inventory would equal 51.3 months’ worth of supply by the end of this year, which was the average level from 2015 to 2021.
The units at NWD’s Austin Bohemian, measuring between 212 sq ft and 380 sq ft, were the city’s first batch of homes that were fully sold out on their debut this year.
They had price tags of between HK$4.92 million and HK$10.17 million, at a maximum discount of 20 per cent. Of the buyers, more than 70 per cent opted to make immediate payment, NWD said.
The largest single buyer spent nearly HK$58 million to purchase nine units, while the priciest transaction translated to about HK$27,000 per square foot.

data.title: Marvel Cosmic Invasion review: Iron Man, Wolverine et al unite for modern beat ’em up
data.description:
Gamers of a certain age will remember when beat ’em ups ruled arcades. Although most arcades are gone, those experiences live on thanks to developers such as Tribute Games, which has made a name for itself by finding new life in the genre.
In 2022, the team released Teenage Mutant Ninja Turtles: Shredder’s Revenge, an excellent adventure inspired by Konami’s Teenage Mutant Ninja Turtles games on the Super Nintendo and in arcades. Now, its follow-up echoes another classic.
Marvel Cosmic Invasion takes the formula from Shredder’s Revenge and applies it to the X-Men arcade game, a legendary beat ’em up that supported up to six players on a two-screen cabinet. That tech was advanced for its time, but nowadays, the widescreen format does not stand out as much.
Instead, Tribute pushes what is capable in the gameplay. The 1990s version had simpler mechanics with three buttons for jumping, attacking and a super move that was powerful but consumed health. Marvel Cosmic Invasion moves beyond that by adding elements of action and fighting games.


When it comes to movement, characters can sprint across the screen with a double-tap. Heroes capable of flying can stay above the fray by hitting the jump button twice. Tribute adds a defensive component with a button that can either dodge or block, depending on the character. With the right timing, fighters such as Phyla-Vell can even counter an enemy attack.
On the offensive side, players have an attack that can be mashed for combos, while the power attack can deliver a ranged blast, grapple move or heavy blow.
What is more, players can hold on to the attack buttons for powered-up attacks. Super moves are still in the Marvel Cosmic Invasion, but instead of shaving off health, they rely on a Focus meter that players build up through fighting.
Tribute adds another wrinkle to the gameplay with the swap button. Before entering a stage, players choose two characters, and they can hit the left trigger to switch to a second hero, similar to a Capcom fighting game.
It is helpful when one hero is being held down by an adversary or if one character is low on health. A tag out can bring a hero with full health into the fore and allow the hurt partner to partially heal up. The swap button can even be used as a finisher for a combo.

Players have plenty of duo combinations to explore with 15 heroes from across the Marvel universe. It is not just mutants. Players can choose Avengers and heroes beyond Earth’s boundaries, such as Nova and Beta Ray Bill. They all join together to fend off an attack from Annihilus and his minions.
Some of the heroes must be unlocked through the campaign, but that is not hard to do. It is best to choose heroes of different talents so that players can deal with a variety of situations. If they encounter a level with a lot of flying foes, it is best to grab someone like Storm or Iron Man, who can hover and take them out.
In addition, each of the heroes will level up after completing a level or being defeated. Hitting a new milestone offers stat perks, passive abilities or a new colour option. That means even if players fail to complete a level, they can keep at it, and the next attempt should be easier with a more powerful hero.
Players can hit a level cap, and it is not hard with 10 levels. Sadly, they all level up the same way without a skill tree or way to make each hero more distinct.

Marvel Cosmic Invasion contains 15 full stages that are drawn beautifully with pixel art. They are well-animated and have a crafted retro charm that will scratch that nostalgic itch.
The joy of these games is the feeling as though you are playing a lost game from your childhood. Tribute mines that vein and takes players to locales that will be familiar to Marvel Cinematic Universe fans as well as comic aficionados.
Unfortunately, not all the levels are well done. Some feel short while others are unimaginative. The best ones have a gimmick or noteworthy layout.
They are all themed well, as the campaign encourages players to pick two canon heroes and complete three objectives for each stage. This gives players cosmic cubes so they can unlock more rewards, which increases the replay value.
Like its arcade brethren, Marvel Cosmic Invasion is not long. Players can complete it over the weekend, but even if they beat it once, there is still plenty to unlock.

Platforms: PlayStation 5, PlayStation 4, Nintendo Switch, Nintendo Switch 2, Xbox Series X and Series S, PC

data.title: Why China Gelao ethnic group required women to have 1 to 2 upper teeth removed before wedding
data.description:
In China’s Gelao ethnic group, an old custom required brides to have one or two upper teeth removed before marriage, supposedly to avoid bringing harm to the groom’s family.
In some cases, the missing teeth were even replaced by those of a dog for decorative purposes.
The Gelao people are an ethnic group in China and Vietnam.
Their estimated population in China in 2021 was more than 677,000.

The group mainly lives in Gelao autonomous counties in the western part of Guizhou province in southern China.
Traditionally, they rely on agriculture, growing rice in flatter terrains and cultivating various grains in the mountainous drylands.
The custom of tooth removal for new brides has been passed down for generations, with the earliest written records of it dating back to the Southern Song dynasty (1127 to 1279).

When a Gelao woman reached around the age of 20 and was preparing for marriage, one or two of her upper front teeth would be intentionally knocked out.
According to a well-known folk legend, many years ago, a Gelao woman fell off a cliff while gathering fruit for her community before her wedding, knocking out her two front teeth.
To honour her bravery and dedication, it became a custom for Gelao brides to have their front teeth removed before marriage.

The procedure followed a specific ritual.
A pot of wine was first prepared and the girl’s maternal uncle was respectfully invited to the home.
Using a small hammer, the uncle would knock out her teeth.
If the uncle was dead or unavailable, another male relative from the mother’s side of the same generation could perform the ritual.
After the teeth were removed, a special medicinal powder was applied to the gums for quick healing.
If a Gelao woman did not undergo the tooth-removal ritual, she could face ridicule or be mocked by the community.
The reasons behind this tooth-knocking custom vary.
Some say it is because the regions inhabited by the Gelao ethnic group had high levels of poison and women who had this would often clench their jaws tightly.
Knocking the teeth out made it easier for them to take herbal remedies.

Others believe that keeping the upper front teeth would bring misfortune to the husband’s family, resulting in a lack of descendants.
To avoid hindering the family’s prosperity, their teeth had to be removed.
However, some say it was purely for aesthetic purposes.
Historical records even say that decorative dog teeth would sometimes be installed as replacements.
According to a 1957 investigation by the Guizhou Provincial Institute of Ethnic Studies, this rather brutal form of body modification was still practised among Gelao communities in areas such as Puding, Zhijin and Renhuai in Guizhou province until the late Qing dynasty (1644–1912), when the tradition gradually began to fade.

data.title: Meet Sarah Sherman, who has a new HBO Max comedy special – and just played a drunk raccoon on SNL
data.description:
Sarah Sherman has a penchant for physical comedy. For Saturday Night Live’s latest Weekend Update, the comedienne dressed up as a drunk raccoon, a pointed reference to the recent news about a furry critter that tore through a Virginia liquor shop and passed out – inebriated – in the bathroom.
The star gave viewers some good entertainment, from taking a dig at Donald Trump by hitting host Colin Jost with a “quiet, piggy” comment – the US president infamously said this to a female Bloomberg News reporter in November – to calling raccoons the “Sarah Shermans of the forest”.


If you want to see more of Sherman, you can tune into her new comedy special, Sarah Squirm: Live + in the Flesh, which just hit HBO Max on December 12. The special, in which legendary filmmaker John Waters makes an appearance, is best described as a psychedelic body horror circus. Sherman told Interview Magazine that it explores the “horrors of having a female body” and is “very sex-negative”.


Keep reading to learn more about Sarah Sherman.
What’s Sarah Sherman’s background?

Sherman hails from Great Neck, New York, where she grew up in a “loud, Jewish, outrageous family”, she told Variety. In a twist of serendipity, Sherman’s bat mitzvah coincided with April Fools’ Day, and she called it a “gift from God”, sending invites through cans filled with snakes, reports Vulture. She did improv and track in school before attending Northwestern University, where she studied theatre.
How did she get into comedy?

“There was never a minute in my life where I didn’t want to be a comedian,” Sherman told Interview Magazine. She got her first open mic gig at The Hog Pit in New York City when she was 16. After graduating from Northwestern, she moved to Chicago.
“I was doing basement shows with bands literally called Piss Piss Piss Moan Moan Moan. I was like, ‘It can’t be Blood Licker, Piss Piss Piss Moan Moan Moan, and Sarah,’” she recalled to Vulture. She eventually launched her own show, Helltrap Nightmare, and later moved to LA. She was discovered by SNL at one of her shows and eventually landed a spot on the iconic programme.
Her political views












View this post on Instagram























A post shared by SARAH SHERMAN (@sarahsquirm)



Sherman was a vocal supporter of Bernie Sanders when he ran for president as a Democratic candidate during the 2020 American presidential election. She once did an impression of him on SNL, too.
Recently, Sherman supported Zohran Mamdani during his bid to become the mayor of New York City. She hosted one of his rallies at Forest Hills Stadium and did a Weekend Update skit parodying his critics. Mamdani became mayor-elect of NYC in November.

data.title: China’s airlines in the hot seat over hidden extra fees as watchdog steps in
data.description:
China’s airlines are encountering severe turbulence over their growing use of extra charges like seat selection fees, as passengers, state media and a consumer protection watchdog slam the practice as a violation of customers’ rights.
The controversy reflects the difficulties Chinese airlines face as they try to strike a delicate balance between profitability and service quality, with an industry insider cautioning that companies may not be able to scrap extra fees completely.
Seat selection fees – long a bugbear of travellers in the West – have become more common in China over recent years, as airlines facing squeezed profit margins seek out extra revenue sources.
But the trend has sparked heated online backlash, leading Chinese state broadcaster CCTV to air a programme criticising the fees and a consumer rights agency to launch an investigation.
The probe by the Jiangsu provincial consumers association found that China’s major state-owned carriers now routinely charge passengers extra to secure a window or aisle seat, or to sit in rows near exits with more legroom.
On average, one-third of economy class seats on the carriers’ flights are unavailable for free selection during online check-in, according to the association. The free seats tend to be in the middle of a row or near the back of the aircraft, where there is more noise and the effects of turbulence are greater.
The watchdog in China’s eastern Jiangsu province met with airline representatives last month to demand they allow passengers to book more seats free of charge.
However, with airlines facing low profitability and passengers having diverse needs, add-on service charges like seat selection fees may need to be optimised rather than abolished, said Jason Zheng, an analyst with Airwefly, a Shanghai-based aviation news portal and consultancy.
“Seat selection fees are an idea that Western firms like United Airlines and Delta Air Lines had decades ago,” Zheng said. “Chinese airlines started charging the fee fairly recently, mostly since the 2010s.”
The practice was more common on long-haul international flights, as passengers were more willing to pay extra for better seats on journeys of 10 hours or more, he added.


For example, none of the seats in the first 10 rows of economy class on a China Southern Airlines flight from Guangzhou to London in November were free to book, with customers needing to pay 500 yuan (US$70) or use membership points to reserve the spots, according to Zheng.
But he stressed that, while consumers should have the freedom to choose, airlines also had to turn a profit.
“When running an airline is no longer a lucrative business, income from add-on services is critical,” Zheng said. “The key is to guarantee consumers have the right to know and choose while letting airlines grow their revenue.”
China is the world’s second-largest aviation market, but cutthroat competition is raging among operators and crimping margins, forcing many to tap alternative income streams.
The country’s “big three” airlines – Air China, China Eastern and China Southern – all reported year-on-year revenue growth in the low single digits for the first three quarters of 2025, with their net profits over the period ranging between 1.9 billion yuan and 2.3 billion yuan.
While the figures look large, in reality the airlines are operating on slender margins given the vast amounts they have to spend on operating and maintenance costs, as well as the purchase of new aircraft, according to Zheng.
When it comes to charging extra fees, Zheng argued a distinction should be made between budget airlines and full-service operators.
“Budget airlines offering low fares can charge reasonable fees for good seats and meals, while full-service firms should make all economy class seats available for selection because passengers pay full prices,” Zheng said.
He suggested that mainstream airlines in China could follow the example of overseas operators by creating a premium economy class to accommodate those willing to pay for extra space and comfort. Pricing, benefits and conditions must be clearly set out, he added.
The companies remain under pressure. The Jiangsu watchdog has vowed to follow up on its probe to ensure the airlines make changes, while also demanding they review their ticketing and service agreements.
State media has also weighed in. CCTV said in a programme aired last month that “first come, first served” was a simple principle that should also apply to how airlines let passengers pick their seats. Any other policy was a violation of consumer rights, it added.

data.title: Record tally of spoiled votes in Hong Kong poll ‘not due to pressure on public’
data.description:
Hong Kong’s constitutional affairs chief has dismissed suggestions that a record number of invalid ballots cast in the recent Legislative Council election was due to some people being forced to vote, adding that authorities may reconsider introducing “smart ballot boxes” to address the issue.
Secretary for Constitutional and Mainland Affairs Erick Tsang Kwok-wai also said on Saturday that last month’s deadly Tai Po fire had affected the voter turnout in the poll.
Last Sunday’s election yielded a turnout rate of 31.9 per cent, up slightly from the record low of 30.2 per cent in the 2021 Legco poll.
But more invalid votes were recorded, with the 41,147 spoiled votes amounting to 3.12 per cent of the total cast. In the 2021 poll, 27,453 were spoiled, accounting for 2.03 per cent of the total.
The government had mounted an all-out push to boost turnout this year until the deadly inferno last month, with the business sector also offering incentives such as a half-day of paid leave and transport subsidies to encourage employees to cast their ballots.




Tsang rejected suggestions that the increase in invalid ballots was a result of people being forced to vote. Instead, he said the void votes could have been due to voters mistakenly choosing more candidates than required, and that authorities would step up measures to reduce such cases.
“For example, two legislators were directly elected in each geographical constituency, and some residents might inadvertently or unknowingly think that they needed to tick two,” he said on a television programme.
“Apart from further strengthening reminders to voters, we will explore measures to minimise the occurrence of this situation.”
He added that authorities would not rule out reconsidering the introduction of “smart ballot boxes” if society accepted it.
Last year, the government shelved the introduction of smart ballot boxes to help electors check the validity of their voting slips after lawmakers raised concerns about privacy and secrecy.
The system would have used a sensor to read the ballot paper to check whether it had been properly marked. If an irregularity was found, the system would immediately alert the voter to review their ballot paper.
The poll was the second since Beijing reformed Hong Kong’s electoral system to ensure only “patriots” can run for political office.
It had 161 candidates competing for 90 seats: 20 from geographical constituencies; 30 from functional constituencies; and 40 from the Election Committee constituency.
The poll was held less than two weeks after the city’s deadliest fire in decades engulfed seven of eight residential buildings at the Wang Fuk Court housing estate in Tai Po, killing at least 160 people.

New Territories North East, which includes the fire-ravaged Tai Po estate, recorded a voter turnout rate of 30.15 per cent – the lowest among the 10 geographical constituencies, where residents directly elected lawmakers.
Tsang said the Tai Po fire had affected the turnout but added that it should not be compared with figures from polls before the electoral system was reformed.
“Some described the turnout rate as the second lowest in history, but I don’t think that comparison is appropriate,” he said.
“We must remember the circumstances of past elections when anti-China and pro-chaos elements entered the legislature through an imperfect electoral system.”
He added: “We certainly hoped that the voting results would improve further. But unfortunately, the fire in Tai Po occurred, which undoubtedly had some impact.”

data.title: Fans vandalise India stadium, throw objects onto field after Lionel Messi’s abrupt exit
data.description:
Angry spectators broke down barricades and stormed the pitch at a stadium in India after football star Lionel Messi, who is on a three-day tour of the country, abruptly left the arena.
As part of a so-called GOAT Tour, the 38-year-old Argentina and Inter Miami superstar touched down in the eastern state of West Bengal early on Saturday, greeted by a chorus of exuberant fans chanting his name.
Hours later, thousands of fans wearing Messi jerseys and waving the Argentine flag packed into Salt Lake Stadium in the state capital, Kolkata, but heavy security around the footballer left fans struggling to catch a glimpse of him.
Messi walked around the pitch waving to fans and left the stadium earlier than expected.
Frustrated fans, many having paid more than US$100 for tickets, ripped out stadium seats and hurled water bottles onto the track.

Many others stormed the pitch and vandalised banners and tents.
“For me, to watch Messi is a pleasure, a dream. But I have missed the chance to have a glimpse because of the mismanagement in the stadium,” businessman Nabin Chatterjee, 37, said.
Another angry fan told the Press Trust of India (PTI) that people had spent “a month’s salary” to see Messi.
“I paid 5,000 rupees (US$55) for the ticket and came with my son to watch Messi, not politicians. The police and military personnel were taking selfies, and the management is to blame,” Ajay Shah told PTI.
A fan at the stadium told Indian news agency ANI: “Only leaders and actors were surrounding Messi … Why did they call us then … We have got a ticket for 12 thousand rupees (US$132.51), but we were not even able to see his face …”

Before the chaos erupted, Messi, who played a friendly match at the Salt Lake stadium in 2011 in which Argentina defeated Venezuela 1-0, unveiled a 21-metre (70-foot) statue that shows him holding aloft the World Cup.
He was also expected to play a short exhibition game at the stadium.
On the tour, Messi is also scheduled to attend concerts, youth football clinics, a Padel tournament and launch charitable initiatives at events in Kolkata, Hyderabad, Mumbai and New Delhi.
The organisers of Messi’s tour of India did not immediately reply to a request for comment.
Kolkata is the capital of the Indian state of West Bengal, which along with the states of Kerala and Goa has long had large football followings in an otherwise cricket-crazed country.
Argentine great Diego Maradona twice visited Kolkata and in 2017 unveiled a statue of himself holding the World Cup, in the presence of thousands of fans.
State chief minister Mamata Banerjee said she was “disturbed” and “shocked” at the mismanagement.

“I sincerely apologise to Lionel Messi, as well as to all sports lovers and his fans, for the unfortunate incident,” she said in a post on X, adding that she had ordered a probe into the incident.
Messi will now head to Hyderabad, Mumbai and New Delhi.
His time in India also includes a possible meeting with Prime Minister Narendra Modi.
Messi won his second consecutive Major League Soccer Most Valuable Player award this week after propelling Inter Miami to the MLS title and leading the league in goals.
The former Barcelona and Paris Saint-Germain attacker will spearhead Argentina’s defence of the World Cup in June-July in North America.

data.title: Temperature in Hong Kong to drop to 14 degrees and remain cool till midweek
data.description:
This story has been made freely available as a public service to our readers. Please consider supporting SCMP’s journalism by subscribing. Get faster notifications on the latest updates by downloading our app.
Temperatures in Hong Kong are set to dip to as low as 14 degrees Celsius (57.2 Fahrenheit) on Sunday after a cold front moves across the coast of Guangdong province, bringing cooler weather until the middle of next week, according to the Observatory.
The weather forecaster said on Saturday that as the cold front moved across the area in the afternoon, local winds would strengthen from the north under the influence of the associated northeast monsoon. There would be a few patches of rain and a significant dip in temperatures, it said.
“The minimum temperature [on Sunday] morning will be around 14 degrees in the urban areas, and it will be cold over parts of the New Territories,” the forecaster said.
“Members of the public are advised to be aware of the change in weather, keep warm and take care of the elderly and persons with chronic medical conditions.”
The northeast monsoon is expected to continue affecting the coast of southern China over the next few days.
The city’s temperature range is expected to be 14 to 20 degrees on Sunday and 16 to 20 degrees on Monday, before climbing back up to about 19 to 25 degrees from Tuesday to Friday.
As the monsoon moderates, the weather will improve over the coast of Guangdong, and temperatures are set to rise gradually. But the temperature difference between day and night will be relatively large over inland areas, according to the Observatory.
A broad band of clouds is expected to bring a few rain patches to southern China towards the end of the week. With the band of clouds thinning out, the weather over the region will be sunnier next weekend, the latest forecast showed.

data.title: China-Africa partnership leaps into the future as Beijing expands market access: envoy
data.description:
The Chinese ambassador to Rwanda said on Friday that China-Africa cooperation had expanded into forward-looking sectors such as artificial intelligence, the digital economy, green energy, fintech and sustainable development.
While attending the Seventh Africa’s Business Heroes Summit held in Kigali, Rwanda, Gao Wenqi thanked entrepreneurs from China and African nations for expanding cooperation into sectors that “reflect the future trends of the global economy”.
“China will continue to broaden market access to Africa,” the envoy said in a speech at the summit on Friday, according to state news agency Xinhua.
Gao said China would share the fruits of its development and vast market with African countries, which would benefit from Beijing’s zero-tariff policy, according to the Chinese embassy in Rwanda.

Africa’s Business Heroes is a competition hosted by Alibaba Philanthropy and the Jack Ma Foundation, and this year was held in partnership with the Rwanda Development Board. The South China Morning Post is owned by Alibaba Group.
“Innovative and hardworking entrepreneurs play a crucial role in China’s growth, and the same holds true for Africa,” Gao said in his speech.
In June, China – the continent’s largest trading partner – announced a zero-tariff policy for products from 53 African countries, excluding eSwatini. The policy is expected to take effect once individual economic partnership agreements are signed with all participating countries.
The summit brought together 1,700 entrepreneurs, investors, policymakers, industry experts and students for discussions on the future of African innovation, according to state news agency Xinhua.
This year’s finalists come from Cameroon, Egypt, Kenya, Rwanda, Senegal, South Africa and Tanzania.
The grand finale of the competition, featuring live pitches from the top 10 finalists, will be held on Saturday. The finalists will share a total of US$1.5 million in grant funding, according to the Rwanda Development Board.
The event was also attended by Sun Lijun, chairman of the board of the Alibaba Foundation and president of Alibaba Philanthropy, who said that Africa’s future would be “shaped by African entrepreneurs”.
“Africa has never lacked entrepreneurial spirit. Africans have never lacked problem-solvers,” Sun said, according to Xinhua.
“Many of Africa’s development challenges can be explored and solved through African innovation. We believe that a new generation of outstanding African entrepreneurs will bring transformative change to Africa,” Sun added.
Gao, the ambassador, said the summit was an inspiring gathering of outstanding entrepreneurs in Africa and a major platform for fostering innovation. He added that past winners had become “role models” for Africa.

data.title: Bump in nostalgia sales, but 183-year Hong Kong umbrella shop set to fold for good
data.description:
At a packed and tiny storefront in Hong Kong’s Sham Shui Po, Yau Yiu-wai busily explains the best way to open and shut umbrellas to an endless stream of interested buyers.
But the 73-year-old shop owner only received a bump in customers after announcing earlier this week that he would be closing down the 183-year-old family business – one of the city’s last remaining umbrella shops with repair services – by the end of this month.
As the fifth-generation owner of the Sun Nga Shing Umbrella Store, Yau said it was a painful decision for him to shut the business, which was established by his ancestors as early as 1842 in Guangzhou during the Qing dynasty.
“The foundation laid by the previous generation was handed down to me, but now it’s ending with me,” Yau told the Post on Saturday.
“I’m unhappy and ashamed, but my body just can’t take it any more. I need to rest.”
Many residents, including neighbours and regular patrons, flocked to the shop to say their goodbyes to Yau shortly after he posted the notice of the coming closure online.
A long-term customer, surnamed Hau, who has lived nearby for over 30 years, said she had bought umbrellas from Yau many times.
“I’m sad to hear [about the closure] after all these years as a neighbour, but it’s normal for him to retire; he’s getting old,” the 60-year-old waitress said.
Yau did not expect such a big reaction, saying his intention was merely to remind customers to pick up their repaired umbrellas – as many as 100 – before he closed the business down.

He set up his own store in Sham Shui Po 45 years ago selling and repairing umbrellas. Though his father’s shop originally sold both umbrellas and general merchandise, he chose to focus solely on umbrellas.
Having grown up watching the umbrella-making process in his grandfather’s shop, Yau said: “I’ve always loved umbrellas, they are works of art, very aesthetically pleasing.”
For years, a seven-day work week was the norm for Yau. He rarely took breaks, resting only for the Lunar New Year holidays or when he had to see a doctor.
Six years ago, Yau suffered a stroke that partially paralysed his body, leading to a three-month hospital stay, followed by another month of recovery at home.
Yau started thinking about retirement five years ago, but letting go of the business was hard. He also believed umbrellas possess sentimental value, and wanted to keep helping customers hold on to their past memories.
“Some were bought while dating, some were bought by parents before they passed away; in short, they all have a story behind them,” he said.
“Few people really think about repairing umbrellas, it takes too much time and makes too little money.”
Yau charges between HK$10 and HK$80 (US$1.30 to US$10.30) for each umbrella repair, depending on the extent of the damage and the time required.
But with the surge of cheap, low-quality umbrellas flooding the market, especially on e-commerce platforms such as Taobao and Pinduoduo, Yau’s business never stood a chance.
“You can buy an umbrella for just over HK$20 [on these platforms], often with free shipping, leaving stores like mine that sell good-quality items with no business,” he said. “Those umbrellas are cheap, but they are not durable.”

Yau’s shop sells imported branded umbrellas, mostly priced between HK$50 and HK$200.
“Business is now less than half of what it was before the [Covid-19] pandemic. It’s a disaster,” he said.
Even though the monthly rent of his shop had recently dropped from HK$8,000 to HK$6,500, Yau said it would not change his mind about shutting the business.
He and his wife have persevered in a family business that has lasted nearly two centuries, but are reluctant to ask their daughter to continue selling umbrellas, which they feel is no longer a viable trade for the next generation.
“It’s useless. You can’t make money and it is too tough,” Yau said.
Yau’s 70-year-old wife, surnamed Ma, said her husband had done his best and deserved to put his feet up after devoting his life to the business.
“Most people retire in their 60s, but he has worked into his 70s. I think he has done more than enough,” she said.
